file_name: Ain et al. - 2024 - Automatically Evolving Interpretable Feature Vectors Using Genetic Programming for an Ensemble Class.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Genetic Programming (GP), an evolutionary computational AI method, to automatically evolve interpretable feature vectors for an ensemble classifier in skin cancer detection. The method was evaluated on two real-world skin image datasets (PH2 and DermoFIT) containing dermoscopy and standard camera images to classify skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel classification approach combining ensemble learning, feature selection, and feature construction using genetic programming. It details the development of a new method (VF-GP), describes experimental evaluations on two benchmark datasets (PH2 and DermoFIT), and reports quantitative results comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art neural networks and other machine learning algorithms. The structure includes an introduction, methodology, experimental results, and discussion of interpretability, which are characteristic of original research.

title: Automatically Evolving Interpretable Feature Vectors Using Genetic Programming for an Ensemble Classifier in Skin Cancer Detection
brief_summary: This study proposes VF-GP, a novel classification approach that combines ensemble learning with genetic programming to automatically evolve variable-length feature vectors for skin cancer detection. The method was evaluated on two real-world datasets, PH2 and DermoFIT, which contain dermoscopy and standard camera images respectively. Experimental results demonstrate that VF-GP significantly outperforms four state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks, two existing GP methods, and eleven commonly used machine learning algorithms. The proposed approach achieved classification performance of 97.80% on the PH2 dataset and 83.31% on the DermoFIT dataset. Furthermore, the study highlights the interpretability of the evolved features, showing they mimic clinical characteristics useful for dermatologists. The method is noted for its low computational cost and potential suitability for mobile devices in low-resource settings.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Common Nevus, Atypical Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer image classification using an ensemble classifier evolved via Genetic Programming. It evaluates performance on two datasets: PH2 (3-class: atypical nevi, common nevi, melanoma) and DermoFIT (10-class: including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, etc.). The primary metric reported is classification accuracy (e.g., 97.80% on PH2, 83.31% on DermoFIT).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy image dataset collected from Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal. It contains images of skin lesions, their binary masks, and clinical diagnoses provided by expert dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.
use.validation: Used for validation of the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.
use.testing: Used for testing the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.

datasets_used.name: DermoFIT Image Library
datasets_used.description: A dataset of standard camera images provided by The University of Edinburgh. It consists of 1300 high-quality skin lesion images captured under standardized conditions. The dataset has no public access.

use.training: Used for training the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.
use.validation: Used for validation of the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.
use.testing: Used for testing the VF-GP ensemble classifier and benchmark methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare VF-GP to other stochastic classification methods (NB, SVM, k-NN, J48, RF, Bagging, AdaBoost, LogitBoost, XGBoost, RC, M3GP) on the PH2 and DermoFIT datasets.
To compare VF-GP to deterministic methods (specifically MLP) on the PH2 and DermoFIT datasets.
To perform a pre-test before applying post-hoc analysis among stochastic methods (VF-GP, MLP, and M3GP).
Applied as a post-hoc test following the one-way ANOVA to account for familywise errors of multiple comparisons when comparing VF-GP to MLP and M3GP.
Set at 0.05 for ANOVA and 0.025 for the post-hoc Bonferroni corrected test to determine statistical significance.
To report the classification performance results across 30 independent GP runs for VF-GP and other methods.

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon signed-rank test, One-sample t-test, One-way ANOVA, and Bonferroni corrected post-hoc test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in classification performance between VF-GP and the compared methods (stochastic or deterministic).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in classification performance between VF-GP and the compared methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test (alpha=0.05) was used to compare VF-GP with stochastic methods, where '+' indicates VF-GP is significantly better. The one-sample t-test was used for deterministic methods (MLP), where '"' indicates VF-GP is significantly better. One-way ANOVA (alpha=0.05) was performed as a pre-test among stochastic methods (VF-GP, MLP, M3GP), followed by a Bonferroni corrected post-hoc test (alpha=0.025) to account for familywise errors. Results showed VF-GP significantly outperformed all compared methods.

PH2.description: 3-class classification (Atypical Nevi, Common Nevi, Melanoma) using dermoscopy images.
PH2.notes: VF-GP with wavelet features achieved the highest accuracy (97.80%). Statistical significance was determined using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (alpha=0.05) for stochastic methods and one-sample t-test for deterministic methods (MLP). VF-GP significantly outperformed all other methods. Post-hoc Bonferroni corrected test (alpha=0.025) confirmed VF-GP was significantly better than MLP (p=2.94395E-16) and M3GP (p=1.059E-22).

DermoFIT.description: 10-class classification (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Intraepithelial carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrhoeic Keratosis, Pyogenic Granuloma, Dermatoﬁbroma, Haemangioma) using standard camera images.
DermoFIT.notes: VF-GP with wavelet features achieved the highest accuracy (83.31%). Statistical significance was determined using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (alpha=0.05) for stochastic methods and one-sample t-test for deterministic methods (MLP). VF-GP significantly outperformed all other methods. Post-hoc Bonferroni corrected test (alpha=0.025) confirmed VF-GP was significantly better than MLP (p=9.0222E-11) and M3GP (p=4.33858E-16). VF-GP achieved 94.07% and 98.38% accuracy on training data for specific evolved individuals using wavelet features.

architecture_used.main_model: VF-GP (Variable-length Feature Genetic Programming) ensemble classifier
architecture_used.input_size: Variable; PH2 dataset images range from 763x553 to 768x576 pixels; DermoFIT dataset images range from 177x189 to 3055x1630 pixels

architecture_details.method_type: Genetic Programming (GP) for automatic feature vector evolution and ensemble classifier training
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Wavelet features, LRGB features, LGray features, and Combined features
ensemble_classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel

benchmark_gp_parameters.m3gp: Existing GP method for feature construction

training_details.validation_strategy: 10-fold cross validation with random stratified sampling
training_details.termination_condition: Ensemble classifier achieves 100% accuracy or maximum 50 generations reached
training_details.benchmark_tool: Waikato Environment for Knowledge Analysis (WEKA) package version 3.8

classification_or_output_head.output: Class label for skin cancer detection (Melanoma, Benign, etc.)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: VF-GP (Variable-length Feature Genetic Programming) ensemble classifier
Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel
conclusions: The proposed VF-GP method significantly outperforms four state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks, existing genetic programming approaches (M3GP), and eleven commonly used machine learning algorithms on both the PH2 and DermoFIT datasets.

VF-GP achieved a classification accuracy of 97.80% on the PH2 dataset and 83.31% on the DermoFIT dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to benchmarks such as MLP, XGBoost, and wrapper classifiers.

The evolved feature vectors are interpretable and mimic clinical characteristics useful for dermatologists, such as texture, color, scale, and frequency-based properties, aiding in the identification of significant visual patterns for skin cancer diagnosis.

The method achieves high classification performance at a low computational cost, with average training times of at most 44.16 seconds for PH2 and 30.43 minutes for DermoFIT, and very low inference times (0.01s and 0.2s respectively).

Due to its low computational resource requirements and fast inference time, the method is potentially suitable for implementation on mobile devices for automated skin lesion screening in low-resource settings.
Wavelet features generally provided the best discrimination ability between classes compared to LRGB and LGray features across both datasets.

Statistical significance tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, one-sample t-test, and Bonferroni corrected post-hoc tests) confirmed that VF-GP's performance improvements over MLP and M3GP are statistically significant.
important_remarks: The DermoFIT dataset has no public access, limiting external validation and reproducibility for the 10-class classification task.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total) and relatively balanced, potentially overestimating performance compared to larger, more imbalanced real-world clinical datasets.

The DermoFIT dataset contains standard camera images with varying sizes and potentially less texture information than dermoscopy images, which may limit the generalizability of the method to dermoscopy-only workflows.

The study highlights that using overall accuracy for imbalanced classification problems leads to bias towards majority class instances, though the proposed method uses balanced accuracy and F1 score to mitigate this.

The method's interpretability relies on evolved features mimicking clinical characteristics (texture, color, scale), but this is qualitative and not validated by dermatologist agreement studies in the provided text.

The training time for the DermoFIT dataset is significantly longer (up to 30.43 minutes) compared to PH2 (44.16 seconds), which may impact deployment feasibility in real-time clinical settings despite low inference time.

The study compares against a limited set of deep learning benchmarks (ResNet-50, AlexNet, 5-layer CNN) and does not include more recent state-of-the-art vision transformers or large-scale foundation models.
